titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 10 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfred Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/52

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
10 Nov My pv. I will try one more letter to Rugby but afterwards until
Saturday I will send them to 29. What will Mater think of your daily post ! ! I know
she will want to hear "all the news." It is a wet day & so I mind the loss that Richardson has
gone off for a long tour investigating a serious charge against the
misuse of the post — luckily not by one of your own Ulster men
but by one of the new people attached to us. I do not think that our morphia magnatewill come out
after all & for once justice may be done though I shall be much
surprised if he does not get a brigade at home in consequence! I
would bet odds on something of this sort. I told you I think that M'Carmmon'sbattalion had been trans-
ferred to the 16th Div as a Reserve. He is awfully upset &I find it
difficult to forgive Gen Powell's obtinacyin Craig's &my advice
to apply for this battalion for our Divn. In the more latter days
he would I feel have taken this advice, but at that time he was
very much the autocrat, as you may remember. How is your cold? I do hope gone. Mine has practically
& in any case it won't do for you to take it to Mater's. She
will probably insist on rubbing you with her rings on , a performance
which I remember even now with some terror ! I am just the
good too much butter & new French bread perhaps but I hope
my tabloids will put this right. The Gen' is distinctly that way
&at meals is very trying sometimes bursting out into a wild
tirade suddenly about the food or waiting. Thank Heavens Farnham
is responsible for the mess,& in consequence it does not react on
him as it does would on any one else who has less intime, or a mere
Commoner ! a very important point. I was next to him last
night & continued my conversation with during this outburst
on purpose — it was a one sided converstion & I am not sure whether
it was approved. was strong on mixing the 16th&36 Div'
but I think I convinced him that he would be making a dangerous
experiment. I asked him what he thought would be the result if some of
one Divn (e g the guns) shot at some of the other, which must often
happen especially at night, or some shot near them, or if one was
a little late in supporting the other, through no fault perhaps, ..., can't you imagine ! This thoughtlessness is so typically the English point of view. Have you read Miss Bates' article in the Nov Nat' Review on
Carson & Ulster. It is very good tho' a little flattering in style. You might
write our joint congratulations if you have time Send it c/o him at the
Ulster club if you wish. I will close now as I think you are rather a spoilt
p r , but you pass on the spoiling too Ever your b w W.BSpender
